responses.response: The teacher shortage is real and binding — we do not dispute this. But A1 frames it as a reason to abandon S3, not to resource it differently. The argument assumes the shortage will not be resolved 'before implementation begins,' but that assumption is a choice about sequencing and investment priority, not a fact about feasibility. If S3 is legislated now with a mandated teacher-retraining and wage-incentive programme beginning immediately (our condition b), then implementation at age 10-14 rollout in 2026-2027 gives 2-3 years for stabilisation. This is not guaranteed — but it is not intrinsically infeasible either. The real question A1 avoids: if we do nothing structural because of teacher shortage, do we solve teacher shortage, or do we just accept it as permanent and design around it? S4 also requires teachers (to deliver the enhanced general-school curriculum), and it requires them in under-resourced schools where shortage is already most acute. S3 at least creates a case for redeployment and wage-lifting. A1's logic — 'the shortage makes reform infeasible, so keep early selection' — is backward: early selection is itself a driver of teacher-shortage concentration in selective schools. We acknowledge this is a hard sequencing problem, not a reason to preserve segregation. The condition that would change us: evidence that a dedicated teacher-retraining programme cannot recruit and stabilise 1,500–2,000 teachers in disadvantaged regions within 24 months. We have not seen that evidence tested.
responses.new_condition: A binding commitment to launch teacher-retraining and wage-incentive programme within 12 months of S3 legislation, with measurable recruitment targets and annual audit. Without this, the shortage risk is real and S3 implementation becomes reckless.

responses.response: A3 names a genuine value conflict: parental choice in track selection vs. equity in track composition. We do not dismiss parental interest — but A3 treats choice and equity as zero-sum, and it treats the current system as the default from which 'choice' is measured. In fact, the current system is already heavily structured by family SES and tutoring capacity, not by parental free choice. A parent in a settlement 40 km from the nearest selective school, with no tutoring resources and no information about selective tracks, does not have 'choice' — they have exclusion presented as non-application. S3 does not eliminate parental choice; it relocates the choice point: instead of choosing a track at age 10 (which most low-SES families cannot meaningfully do), parents can influence differentiation pathways at age 14 (after academic stabilisation). Middle-class parents will still have resources to push for academically intensive instruction — but they will do so alongside peers of different backgrounds, not in a parallel track. The fear A3 names is real: parents of academically strong children worry their child will be 'held back.' But this is an assumption about within-school mixed-ability teaching, not an inevitable outcome of comprehensive schooling. The condition that would change us: evidence from a rigorous trial that within-school differentiation in a comprehensive setting (with diagnostic support and teacher training) produces lower achievement for high-ability low-SES pupils than tracked separation does. We have not seen that evidence. What we have seen is that tracked separation produces lower achievement for high-ability low-SES pupils than for tracked high-ability high-SES pupils — which is precisely why we oppose it.
responses.new_condition: Parental choice for track intensity is legitimate, but it must be informed choice (not hidden by information barriers) and it must not be convertible into permanent segregation. S3 can accommodate this if diagnostic support systems allow parents to request intensity pathways at 14 based on demonstrated ability, not prior family status.

responses.response: A5 argues that Portugal's non-structural investment path works and transfers to Hungary. We agree Portugal achieved gains — but A5 elides a critical difference: Portugal did not select as early or as severely as Hungary. Portugal's 10th-grade tracking (age 15–16) is later than Hungary's age-10/12 selection, and it sorted fewer pupils into vocational tracks. TEIP funding worked in a context where the sorting had already happened less brutally. Hungary's A5-equivalent path (S4: keep age-10/12 selection, fund general schools better) is not the Portugal case — it is a more severe segregation regime attempting compensation. The scenario itself acknowledges this: 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age.' Exactly. A5's transferability assumption is the weak link. We also note A5 contains a political observation: 'without a visible flagship result, the investment path is politically fragile and gets cut.' This is true — and it is precisely why S4 is a dead end. The visible flagship result in the current Hungarian system is selective schools; general schools remain residual in public perception. Even with better funding, S4 cannot change that perception while the selection mechanism remains. The result is that TEIP-style funding is the first thing cut in fiscal consolidation (the scenario flags 'exposure is serious'). The condition that would change us: a rigorous trial in a Hungarian jurisdiction where S4 is implemented in full (with binding multi-year funding guarantees) and is compared to a contemporaneous S3 pilot in a similar jurisdiction, measured over 8 years on equity and mean outcomes. Until then, we cannot distinguish Portugal-transfer from SES-compensation-ceiling.
responses.new_condition: S4 becomes acceptable as a transitional bridge only if paired with explicit S3 legislation tabled within 24 months. Without that pairing, the 'visible flagship' is the persistent selection system, and compensation funding becomes a legitimation mechanism for segregation, not a reform.

responses.response: A8 is the strongest argument against S3 on its merits. Finland's equity gains came from a bundle: comprehensive structure (age 7–16), teacher professionalism and autonomy (not scripted curriculum), universal support (speech therapy, special education embedded, not segregated), and high social trust. A8 correctly observes that structure alone does not deliver those gains — and A8 correctly fears that adopting only the structural piece without the bundle will fail and damage reform credibility. We do not contest this. But A8's conclusion — that we should therefore not attempt S3 — is a non-sequitur. The alternative to 'doing S3 incompletely' is not 'doing nothing structural'; it is 'doing S3 completely.' This requires conditions (our c and d: universal diagnostic support systems, teacher-retraining bundle). These are hard and expensive — but they are the only route to an equity gain. A8's implicit claim is: 'since the full bundle is hard, keep early selection.' But early selection does not free us from the bundle — it only makes the bundle irrelevant because the segregation is already locked in. We acknowledge A8 points to a real risk: rolling out S3 without diagnostic support and teacher retraining will produce within-school tracking that recreates segregation. This is the fate that befell many countries (including some post-1989 EU accessions) that adopted comprehensive structure without the supporting institutions. The condition that would change us: if we observe that teacher-retraining and diagnostic-support capacity cannot be built within the 24-month window, or if a pilot shows that within-school differentiation cannot be audited and managed (our condition g), then S3 becomes reckless and we would revise to conditional support, pending those capacities. But the absence of capacity is not an argument for early selection; it is an argument for building capacity differently.
responses.new_condition: S3 becomes defensible only if implemented as the full bundle: structure + teacher professionalism + universal support + diagnostic systems. A pilot implementation without all four components is a test of the bundle, not of S3 itself, and should be evaluated accordingly. If the bundle cannot be funded, then S3 is deferred, not abandoned — the alternative to a well-resourced reform is not a poorly-resourced reform, but a clear political commitment to resource it.
